ORIGINALITY.justification: The paper presents a notable and concrete novelty: training pointer-network style sequence models for combinatorial optimization with policy gradients (Section 4) rather than the supervised setup of Vinyals et al., and it introduces the Active Search procedure that optimizes the policy on a single test instance (Section 4.1). It also augments training with an actor-critic baseline and shows application beyond TSP (Section 6.1, Knapsack), clearly differentiating from prior supervised pointer-network work and much older neural heuristics. The contribution is a non-trivial and informative combination of existing building blocks (pointer networks, policy gradients, actor-critic) that would be new and useful to experts, though not a radical theoretical advance.

CLARITY.justification: The paper is well-organized with a clear logical flow from introduction through method, experiments and conclusion, and the main contributions (Neural Combinatorial Optimization with RL pretraining and Active Search) are stated early in the introduction. Notation for the TSP objective and policy factorization is mostly consistent and the algorithms (Alg.1/2) and architecture details (pointer network, attention/glimpse) help comprehension. Figures/tables are referenced and the experimental setup/results are described in detail, but the provided text contains several typos and minor presentation issues (e.g. “isssues”, “availabe”, “udpates”, “non-parameteric”), some duplicated/malformed passages and a few places where figures/tables are not shown, which slightly reduces polish and clarity.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: The core technical claims are clearly stated and supported by experiments: the REINFORCE/actor-critic derivations (Eqns. 3–6) and the active-search scheme are mathematically correct and the optimization algorithms are standard and applied correctly. Empirically they compare to reasonable baselines (Christofides, OR-Tools, Concorde/LK-H) on 1,000-instance test sets and report runtimes and solution quality, supporting the claim of near-optimal performance given sufficient search. Minor issues: supervised baseline results are not fully reimplemented (they defer some comparisons to prior work), large sampling budgets (e.g. 1.28M samples per instance) make some results computationally expensive, and no statistical variability/significance measures are reported; these affect evaluation detail but not the main technical validity. Overall the paper is technically sound with only minor experimental-reporting shortcomings.
